name: Nazca Mummies
description: The 'Nazca Mummies' refer to a series of mummified remains discovered near Nazca, Peru, beginning around 2016. These remains, often featuring elongated skulls and three fingers and toes, gained notoriety due to claims suggesting they represent a previously unknown humanoid species, possibly of extraterrestrial origin. Proponents argue that the unusual morphology, DNA analysis, and carbon dating results support their non-human nature or significant genetic manipulation. However, mainstream science strongly rejects these claims. Scholarly analysis indicates that the bodies are likely constructed from the remains of various animals and humans, meticulously assembled to create the appearance of novel beings. Carbon dating discrepancies and manipulated DNA results have further fueled skepticism. The claims are largely based on interpretations by individuals outside of mainstream scientific institutions, often lacking peer-reviewed publications. Archaeological context is poorly documented due to the circumstances of discovery (looting), making proper scientific investigation challenging. The controversy highlights the dangers of misinformation and the importance of rigorous scientific methodology when dealing with potentially significant archaeological finds. The narrative surrounding the Nazca mummies has become intertwined with various alternative history narratives and UFOlogy, reinforcing pre-existing beliefs about ancient aliens and suppressed archaeological discoveries. The ethical implications of displaying and studying potentially desecrated human remains are also a key concern.
subtitle: Alleged non-human remains from Nazca.

claims.claim: The mummies represent a new, non-human species.
claims.evidence: Unusual skeletal structure (elongated skulls, three fingers/toes), DNA analysis suggesting unknown sequences.
claims.criticism: Skeletal anomalies are attributed to artificial cranial deformation and possible assembly of different remains. DNA analysis has been questioned for contamination and manipulation. Initial claims of unknown DNA sequences have not been substantiated by independent, peer-reviewed research.

claims.claim: Carbon dating proves the mummies are extremely old, predating known human civilizations in the area.
claims.evidence: Initial carbon dating results presented by proponents.
claims.criticism: Carbon dating results are inconsistent and contested. Independent analysis suggests the remains are relatively recent, possibly dating back to the pre-Columbian or colonial period. The integrity of the samples used for dating has been questioned.

claims.claim: The mummies possess advanced technology or biological features beyond human capabilities.
claims.evidence: Claims of metallic implants or unusual tissue composition.
claims.criticism: Alleged implants are likely artifacts or natural mineral formations. Claims of unusual tissue composition lack robust scientific evidence and peer-reviewed validation. No credible evidence of advanced technology has been presented.

keyFigures.name: Thierry Jamin
keyFigures.role: One of the main proponents and researchers of the Nazca mummies.

keyFigures.name: Jose Benitez
keyFigures.role: Another prominent figure involved in the study and promotion of the mummies.

keyFigures.name: Flavio Estrada
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who has publicly debunked the claims about the mummies.

mythologyConnections: Possible misinterpretation of deities or mythological figures with unusual features

timeline.event: Discovery and initial claims about the Nazca mummies begin circulating.
Continued debate and conflicting analyses regarding the nature of the remains.

sources.author: National Geographic
sources.work: Various articles and documentaries covering the controversy.
sources.title: National Geographic
